definition: A method that seems more interested in a class other than the one it actually is in, accessing its data more than its own.
ruleOfThumb: If a method uses more features of another class than its own, it probably wants to live there.

citation.author: Martin Fowler
citation.bookTitle: Refactoring: Improving the Design of Existing Code

questions.prompt: What's the main problem with the calculateShippingCost method?

options.text: The method pulls almost all of its data from Order rather than working with its own state
options.explanation: calculateShippingCost calls five different getters on Order and uses none of ShippingCalculator's own fields. A method this focused on another class's data probably belongs in that class.
A long parameter list might be a concern in computeRate, but that's a separate issue. The core problem here is where all that data is coming from — another class.

options.text: The method is doing too many things at once
options.explanation: This method is fairly focused — it gathers data and delegates to computeRate. The problem isn't how much it does, but whose data it's borrowing to do it.

options.text: Order exposes too many public getters
options.explanation: Wide getter surfaces can be a design concern, but they're a symptom here, not the cause. The real issue is that ShippingCalculator is consuming all of them — suggesting this logic belongs inside Order.

questions.prompt: This Invoice method doesn't quite fit. What gives it away?

options.text: The method does all its work with Customer's data and never touches Invoice's own fields
options.explanation: generateTaxSummary ignores this.items and this.issuedAt entirely — everything it needs comes from Customer. That's a strong signal the method belongs in Customer, not Invoice.

options.text: Using a static TaxRules class instead of an instance is the problem
options.explanation: Delegating to a static rules class is a legitimate pattern for pure calculation logic. The issue isn't how the tax is computed — it's that the method lives in the wrong class.

options.text: Returning a complex object (TaxSummary) instead of a primitive value is bad practice
options.explanation: Returning rich objects is generally a good practice — it avoids primitive obsession. The return type isn't the problem here.

options.text: Invoice has too many responsibilities overall
options.explanation: We can only see one method, so there's no evidence the class has too many responsibilities. Focus on what this method is doing and whose data it's depending on.

questions.prompt: Both snippets calculate a discount. Which version has the more problematic design?

options.text: Version A — it reaches into ShoppingCart and its owner to gather data for a calculation that belongs closer to that data
options.explanation: Version A pulls four pieces of data out of ShoppingCart (items, subtotal, owner, loyalty points) — everything it needs lives there. The method should move into ShoppingCart or its owner. Version B already does this: it lives in ShoppingCart and delegates only one call to owner.

options.text: Version B — mixing cart logic with owner data inside the same method violates separation of concerns
options.explanation: Version B touches owner once to get tenure data — that's reasonable delegation, not a violation. It otherwise works with its own fields (this.subtotal, this.discountRate). Compare that to Version A, which needs four things from a class it doesn't belong to.
